screens.prompt: You record the burst once, capturing one long timeline of amplitude readings. Have you now captured everything there is to know about the random process generating it — or is your single recording just one possible outcome among many the same process could have produced?
choices: One recording is the whole story — the process is whatever that recording shows
One recording is just one realization; the process itself is the whole collection of realizations it could have produced
screens.reveal: One recording is just a single **realization** (or *sample path*) of the underlying **random process**. Formally, a random process $X(t)$ is a family of random variables, one for every time $t$ — at each instant, $X(t)$ has some distribution, and a full realization is one particular outcome drawn simultaneously across all of those instants. Your recording is one thread through an entire fabric of equally-possible timelines; the statistics you actually want (mean, autocorrelation) are properties of the whole fabric, called **ensemble statistics**, not just properties you can read off a single thread.

screens.prompt: You imagine re-running the burst's recording five separate times (five realizations), and at exactly $t = 2$s the five readings are $1, 3, 2, 3, 1$. What is the ensemble average $E[X(2)]$ at that instant?
screens.successFeedback: Right — (1+3+2+3+1)/5 = 10/5 = 2. This is exactly what 'ensemble average at time t' means: average across many parallel realizations at one fixed instant, not across time within a single realization.
hints: Add up all five readings, then divide by the number of realizations (5).

screens.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes an ensemble average from a time average?

choices.text: An ensemble average is across many separate realizations at one fixed time; a time average is across many time samples within one single realization
They are just two names for the exact same computation
choices.feedback: They generally compute different things: one averages 'across parallel universes' at a fixed instant, the other averages 'along one timeline' over many instants. They only coincide under a special property called ergodicity — the next lesson.

choices.text: Ensemble averages only apply to discrete-time processes
choices.feedback: Ensemble averaging applies equally to continuous-time and discrete-time processes — the distinction is about averaging across realizations versus across time, not about the time axis being discrete or continuous.

screens.successFeedback: Exactly — and you only ever actually have one realization (your one recording), so you'll need a very good reason to trust that a time average computed from it stands in for the true ensemble average. That reason is exactly what the ergodicity lesson supplies.

screens.body: Two ensemble statistics matter most: the **mean function** $\mu_X(t) = E[X(t)]$ (the ensemble average at each instant $t$, which can in general be different at every $t$), and the **autocorrelation function** $R_X(t_1, t_2) = E[X(t_1)X(t_2)]$ (how the process at one instant relates, on average across the ensemble, to the process at another instant). Notice both are defined with two full arguments in general — $\mu_X$ depending on $t$, and $R_X$ depending on *both* $t_1$ and $t_2$ separately, not just their difference. That generality is deliberate: nothing so far assumes these functions simplify to anything nicer. The next lesson introduces exactly the special case where they do.
screens.selfExplainPrompt: In your own words: why does the autocorrelation function need two separate time arguments, t₁ and t₂, rather than just one?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: A strong answer notes that R_X(t₁,t₂) = E[X(t₁)X(t₂)] measures how the process's values at two potentially different instants relate to each other on average — that's inherently a comparison between two points in time, so it needs two independent time arguments to express fully, unlike the mean function μ_X(t) which only ever looks at one instant at a time.

screens.worked: Across four realizations, at $t_1=1$s the readings are $2, 4, 2, 4$ and at $t_2=3$s (same four realizations, matched up) the readings are $3, 1, 3, 1$. The autocorrelation $R_X(1,3) = E[X(1)X(3)]$ is computed realization by realization then averaged: $\frac{(2)(3)+(4)(1)+(2)(3)+(4)(1)}{4} = \frac{6+4+6+4}{4} = \frac{20}{4} = 5$.
screens.prompt: Across four realizations, at $t_1=0$s the readings are $1, 2, 1, 2$ and at $t_2=5$s (same four realizations) the readings are $3, 3, 5, 5$. What is $R_X(0,5)$?
screens.successFeedback: Right — pairing matched realizations: (1)(3)+(2)(3)+(1)(5)+(2)(5) = 3+6+5+10 = 24, divided by 4 realizations gives 6.
hints: Multiply the matched pair from each realization together, then average all four products.
Realization pairs: (1,3), (2,3), (1,5), (2,5). Multiply each pair, sum, divide by 4.
